summary: McKenzie teases his next software project, which would be significantly more ambitious than Bingo Card Creator, targeting a small but growing community of less price-sensitive customers. He plans a multi-tier pricing model including freemium and subscription options, while keeping costs extremely low by reusing infrastructure from his first product. The timeline is delayed until January because he's studying for the JLPT Level 1 Japanese proficiency exam. He acknowledges the risk of depending on a third-party platform but remains enthusiastic about the planning process.
key_points: The new project targets a niche community of ~4,000 users experiencing rapid growth, with some prospects handling near $500K — a fundamentally different market than Bingo Card Creator's price-sensitive audience
He plans a sophisticated pricing strategy with four tiers (free, freemium, premium, premium + subscription), recognizing the product screams for a SaaS model
Bootstrap economics remain central: $500 budget for six months by reusing learnings and assets from Bingo Card Creator
He identifies platform dependency as the key risk — a third party could fail or eliminate the pain points he plans to solve
The extended JLPT digression reveals how deeply embedded he is in Japan, navigating professional life while functionally illiterate in written Japanese
money_quotes: There is no possible way I can or would do the 'full time job on top of a full time job' deathmarch for that long — it nearly hospitalized me the first time.
I think I can do this for probably $500 for the first 6 months.
This application screams 'Make part of me a web service and charge by the month.'
I'd be scarily dependent on a third-party not totally wrecking me by either failing or radically altering their own software/business to eliminate the pains I'm going to solve.
I'm going to get JLPT level 1 this year or perish in the attempt.
Try to imagine doing the same as a grown professional in real time with the boss standing over your shoulder.
Level 1: 'I can read Kirin's annual shareholder guidance and scoff at their inadequate protection against currency fluctuations.'
key_insight: Even in 2006, McKenzie was already thinking in terms of SaaS pricing tiers and platform risk — foundational business strategy concepts that most indie developers wouldn't internalize for years.
